transcript.text: ...and is obviously enjoying his unscheduled holiday in the Western Isles.
And that's the news on Radio 1 at 10.02.
On 275 and 285, also in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Oh, hello there. It's not Smokey the Bear, it's Tommy Vance.
And welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.
And this week we continue to replay the events as they happened at this year's Reading Festival.
Tonight, you'll be able to hear Def Leppard and Gillen as they were in our series that we call Live from Reading.
We've got classic cuts by Peter Gabriel and Greg Allman, among others, and new releases by Vardis, Samson, David Bowie, and the Scorpions.
Not forgetting our Friday Night Connection.
I answered the last week's one that was set by some strange fellow called Tommy Vance.
This evening, we start with Gamma.
...from one of his albums called Peter Gabriel.
That's indeed all his albums called Peter Gabriel.
And here comes the blood.
But especially for a recently born nephew of Neil Grant,
who lives in Fife, a young man by the name of Andrew Grant.
P.S., says Neil, who signs himself your warped and obedient servant,
Avril Alexander has a wondrous nose.
By the way, you heard something from a new album by a relatively new band.
It's their second album.
The band are called Gamma.
The album's called Gamma 2.
And the track that we played was called Mean Street.
And that was for Donna, Keith, Chris, Solo, Biff, Edge,
and everybody who goes to Mr. M.
Very, very best wishes from Liz Glover,
who also wants me to say a special hello to her boyfriend, Neil,
who keeps nagging me because I never saw Floyd at Earl's Court in Egypt.
Maybe now he'll shut up.
Who knows?
And also it was for Claire and Lisa and Vivian of Worcester,
whom it says on this bit of paper,
we met on halls down Difford Way
from Richard David and St. John.
And here's Vardis.
Absolute piece of recorded magic
from one of the finest albums that's ever been released.
Why do I say that? Because it's true.
And it's also from an album called Metal Explosion,
a Friday rock show which has just come out on BBC Records.
The band you heard there was Samson,
and the track was Takin' Like a Man.
Order one coffee now.
For each member of your family.
And prior to that, you heard a new single by Vardis,
which is out on Logo, the single's called Let's Go.
And incidentally, when you get that single, you get a free one as well, called 100MPH.
Lovely letter here from Ray Purvis, who lives at 106 Hot Chill View, Denny in Stirlingshire.
Dear TV, what can I say?
I've recently bought a stereo FM receiver, and to test it tuned to your Friday night show.
There aren't enough adjectives to describe the quality of your show.
Sheer magic is all that I can think of.
For Ray, if it's down to the FM quality, it's down to the BBC engineers, and they know what they're doing.
You ought to mention for Marion, who's the love of your life,
also for Gabe, the rock culture, and Jim France, the TV heavy.
But I was to dedicate something here from Helen Ozzie Gray,
who lives in Western Boulevard in Nottingham.
I was going to dedicate something to a gentleman by the name of Mick,
who's very ill, it seems, with a filthy animal tailor-style complaint.
And I don't know what that means either.
And also to his loyal friend, Florence Doghead Nightingale,
and everybody else who goes to Ellis Guilford Comprehensive.
10, 23 and a half, and here's David Bowie.
I was actually having a chat with him yesterday,
and you'll hear the outcome of that on Rock on Saturday in a couple of weeks' time.
That was Greg Allman from his album that came out in 73.
The album was called Laid Back, and the track, of course, was Midnight Rider.
And I even remember loving the reggae version of that.
That was done by who? Paul Davidson or somebody like that.
Great song. Do anything with it.
Prior to that, you heard David Bowie and a track from his new album.
The album's called Scary Monsters.
And the track you heard was Teenage Wildlife.
25 to 11, let's now have some more from our live at Reading series.
Ladies and gentlemen, here are Def Leppard.
That was Def Leppard as they were on stage at the recent Reading Festival.
The tracks you heard were Get Your Rocks Off, Ladies Strange, Overture, Medicine Man,
The Walls Came Tumbling Down, coupled with Satellite,
The sound mix was by Dave Dade, and the production was by Tony Wilson.
Later this evening, we have Gillan from the same stage.
Three minutes after 11 o'clock, it's time for the Friday Night Connection, here on the Friday Rock Show.
Now, the tracks that I played you last week were the ones that I dug out of the library,
because I put the connection together last week.
A bit selfish, but then I liked the music.
Tracks I played were Smokin' by Boston, Friction by Television, and Movin' in Stereo by The Cars.
Simple answer.
All the tracks are from the debut album of all those bands.
And the guy that gets the £10 record token is Stuart Campbell.
I think I ought to give him a sort of plaid one.
He lives at 19 Dukes Road, Cambuslang, Glasgow, G727BA.
Stuart, congratulations. £10 record token's on its way to you.
As everybody knows, part of the deal is to put together a Friday night connection yourself.
And that's exactly what Stuart has done.
Three bits of music with a connection in there somewhere.
A Friday Night Connection tonight, put together by Stuart Campbell,
19 Dukes Road, Cambuslang in Glasgow.
Three bits of music, definite connection in there somewhere.
If you think you know what it is, jot it down on a communique and send it to
The Friday Night Connection, The Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4WW.
If you're right, you get a £10 record token.
Say that again, a £10 record token.
Don't forget to put together your own Friday Night Connection as well.
Listening to that man playing the guitar reminds me to tell you
that there's going to be a tribute to Jimi Hendrix in rock on Saturday.
Not tomorrow, but the Saturday following.
That's not to be missed.
This is the Friday Rock Show.
It's 11.17 now, and we return to our live from Reading stage.
And this time, get ready for 38 minutes of mind-boggling good music
blasting into your ears by Gillum.
A song from last year.
They'd had the Reading Festival, and the tracks he heard were
Smoke on the Water, Vengeance, Trouble, If You Believe Me,
Mr. Universe and Unchain Your Brain.
A quick reminder that Gillen are going to go out
on the United Kingdom tour soon.
Now, that incidentally was on-site produced by Tony Wilson,
but the whole 24 track was mixed by Gillen himself,
specially for you.
Next week, in the same slot, we have Budgie and Iron Maiden.
And to take us home, the Scorpions and the Zoo.
The Scorpions and their current single, which is called The Zoo,
and that's specially for everybody at Saltcoats and Stevenson,
the Headbangers, that is,
and everybody who attends St. Andrew's Academy
from Andrew Clarke, who lives in Ayrshire in Scotland.
And that's it for this edition of the Friday Rock Show,
which we trust you enjoyed.
On Rock on Saturday tomorrow, between 4 and 7.30,
we have Echo and the Bunnymen, Paul McCartney,
and some tracks from the new Supertramp double album,
the live one that's coming out soon.
Many thanks to our technical operator tonight, Ian.
Thank you, Ian.
Tony Wilson, our producer, will not be coming to address the microphone tonight.
Well, at least not from this distance,
because I've got a stinking cold.
But you might shout from the back of the hall.
Oh, yes, I will.
I'm not afraid of a few germs.
Nigel!
No, no.
Shoot, thank you.
Following the pips,
the Radio 1-275-285 transmitters
will be closing down
and they'll be back with you on the air
tomorrow morning at 5 o'clock.
So on behalf of all of us on Radio 1,
this is TV on the Radio Thomas fans
wishing you well wherever you are.
Take care of yourselves.
God bless and good night.
